draft.introduction: 1 Timothy 1:1–17 opens with warnings against false teachers and concludes with Paul’s autobiographical confession of being the “foremost of sinners” who received mercy. From Codex Sinaiticus, fourth century.

translation.text: As I urged you when I was going to Macedonia, remain at Ephesus so that you may charge certain persons not to teach any different doctrine, nor to devote themselves to myths and endless genealogies, which promote speculations rather than the stewardship from God that is by faith. The aim of our charge is love that issues from a pure heart and a good conscience and a sincere faith.
translation.original_text: καθως παρεκαλεσα σε προσμειναι εν εφεσω

translation.text: The saying is trustworthy and deserving of full acceptance, that Christ Jesus came into the world to save sinners, of whom I am the foremost. But I received mercy for this reason, that in me, as the foremost, Jesus Christ might display his perfect patience as an example to those who were to believe in him for eternal life. To the King of the ages, immortal, invisible, the only God, be honor and glory forever and ever. Amen.
translation.original_text: πιστος ο λογος και πασης αποδοχης αξιος

translator_notes.note: The warning against “myths and endless genealogies” may target early Gnostic speculation about chains of divine emanations (aeons). The Valentinian system elaborated exactly such genealogies of divine beings. Whether the Pastorals address developed Gnosticism or proto-Gnostic tendencies remains debated.
